title: Vanishing & Exploding Gradients
tagline: Where the magic 0.25 comes from — and what stops it from ruining deep networks
summary: Stack a hundred layers; multiply a hundred Jacobians; watch the gradient either die or blow up. A single-layer derivative panel demystifies the magic factor (0.25 for sigmoid, 1.0 for ReLU); a depth slider shows the multiplicative chain compounding; a weight-scale slider switches the failure mode from vanishing to exploding. Then the misconceptions card cleans up what 'vanishing' actually means.
highlights: Single-layer derivative explorer: drag z across [-6, 6] and watch σ'(z) move; immediately see why max σ' = 0.25 for sigmoid.
Numeric chain: 0.25 × 0.25 × ... × 0.25 written out term by term so 'multiplicative collapse' is visceral, not abstract.
Weight-scale slider exposes exploding gradients — the symmetric failure mode that fixes the same way (init + norm).
ReLU's 'dying neurons' separated from the vanishing story: linear scaling, not exponential collapse.
Misconception cards address the three things students consistently get wrong about vanishing gradients.
ES 667 Lec 1–2 demo (depth, init, vanishing); pairs with the autograd article for the underlying chain rule.
